audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Levite and unnamed concubine, Bethlehem delay, Gibeah hospitality, sexual assault, death, dismemberment, and summons narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for pilegesh status, MT/Greek estrangement variant, paternal-house delay, travel and hospitality geography, Gibeah's refusal of shelter, threatened sexual violence against male guest, host's offer of daughter and concubine, Levite's surrender of the woman, prolonged gang rape, threshold death, silencing command, postmortem dismemberment, circulation of body parts, and national outrage framing.

verses.restored: In those days, when there was no king in Israel, there was a Levite sojourning in remote parts of hill country of Ephraim. He took for himself a woman, a concubine, from Bethlehem in Judah.

His concubine became estranged from him—Masoretic Hebrew uses a verb often read as sexual unfaithfulness, while Greek witnesses read that she became angry with him—and she went away from him to house of her father, to Bethlehem in Judah. She was there four months of days.

Her man/husband arose and went after her, to speak to her heart and bring her back. His servant was with him and a pair of donkeys. She brought him into house of her father, and father of young woman saw him and rejoiced to meet him.
His father-in-law, father of young woman, took hold of him, and he dwelt with him three days. They ate and drank and lodged there.
On fourth day they arose early in morning, and he rose to go. Father of young woman said to his son-in-law, Strengthen your heart with a morsel of bread, and afterward you may go.
The two sat and ate and drank together. Father of young woman said to the man, Be willing, please, and lodge, and let your heart be good.
The man rose to go, but his father-in-law urged him, and he returned and lodged there.
He arose early in morning on fifth day to go. Father of young woman said, Strengthen your heart, please, and linger until day declines. The two of them ate.

The man rose to go, he and his concubine and his servant. His father-in-law, father of young woman, said to him, Look, please, day has weakened toward evening. Lodge, please; look, day is encamping. Lodge here, and let your heart be good. Tomorrow arise early for your way, and go to your tent.
The man was not willing to lodge. He arose and went and came opposite Jebus, that is Jerusalem. With him were a pair of saddled donkeys, and his concubine was with him.
They were near Jebus, and day had gone down greatly. The servant said to his master, Come, please, and let us turn aside to this city of Jebusites and lodge in it.
His master said to him, We will not turn aside to a city of foreigners who are not from children of Israel; we will pass on to Gibeah.
He said to his servant, Come, and let us draw near to one of the places, and we will lodge in Gibeah or in Ramah.
They passed on and went, and sun came down upon them beside Gibeah, which belongs to Benjamin.
They turned aside there to enter and lodge in Gibeah. He entered and sat in open square of city, but no one gathered them into a house to lodge.
Look, an old man came from his work from field in evening. The man was from hill country of Ephraim, and he sojourned in Gibeah; people of place were Benjaminites.
He lifted his eyes and saw the traveling man in open square of city. The old man said, Where are you going, and from where do you come?

He said to him, We are passing from Bethlehem in Judah to remote parts of hill country of Ephraim; from there I am. I went to Bethlehem in Judah, and I am going to house of YHWH, but no one gathers me into a house.
There is both straw and fodder for our donkeys, and there is bread and wine for me, for your maidservant, and for the young man with your servants. There is no lack of anything.
The old man said, Peace be to you. Only let all your lack be upon me; only do not lodge in open square.
He brought him into his house and mixed feed for donkeys. They washed their feet and ate and drank.

While they were making their heart good, look, men of city, sons of beliyyaal/lawlessness, surrounded the house, beating upon door. They said to old man, master of house, Bring out the man who came into your house, so we may sexually know him.
The man, master of house, went out to them and said to them, No, my brothers, please do not act wickedly. After this man has come into my house, do not commit this outrage.
Look, my daughter, a virgin, and his concubine. Let me bring them out, please. Violate them and do to them what is good in your eyes; but against this man do not commit this outrageous thing.

The men were not willing to listen to him. The man seized his concubine and brought her out to them outside. They sexually knew her and abused her all night until morning, and they sent her away when dawn rose.
The woman came toward turning of morning and fell at entrance of house of the man where her lord was, until light.
Her lord arose in morning, opened doors of house, and went out to go on his way. Look, the woman, his concubine, was fallen at entrance of house, and her hands were upon threshold.
He said to her, Arise, and let us go. There was no answer. He took her upon donkey, and the man arose and went to his place.
He came to his house, took the knife, seized body of his concubine, and cut her limb by limb into twelve pieces. He sent her throughout all territory of Israel.
Everyone who saw said, Such a thing has not happened and has not been seen from day children of Israel came up from land of Egypt to this day. Set your heart upon it; take counsel and speak.

translation_notes: The woman is never named. 'Concubine' translates pilegesh, a recognized but structurally subordinate partner status. That status never reduces her humanity, consent, bodily autonomy, or right to safety.

Verse 2 has a major witness difference. Masoretic Hebrew uses zanah, often read as sexual unfaithfulness, while Greek witnesses describe anger or estrangement. Neither reading causes, excuses, or morally prepares the violence later committed against her.

The Levite goes 'to speak to her heart,' language of persuasion or reconciliation, but the narrative never records her speech or explicit consent to return. Her father's hospitality cannot substitute for her decision.

The Levite refuses a Jebusite city because its residents are not Israelites and chooses Gibeah. The resulting atrocity deliberately destroys any claim that in-group identity guarantees moral safety or that foreigners are inherently more dangerous.
Gibeah's failure begins before the assault: travelers sit in the square without welcome. Hospitality is not merely private courtesy here; social abandonment leaves vulnerable people exposed.

The mob demands sexual domination of the male guest. The host condemns this while offering his daughter and the concubine instead. Protecting one person by surrendering women to rape is itself monstrous, not hospitality or lesser harm.

Verse 24 preserves the host's words, 'do what is good in your eyes,' echoing the book's moral refrain in a scene where patriarchal judgment treats women's bodies as negotiable. The text reports this collapse; it does not endorse it.
Verse 25 states that the Levite seizes and puts his concubine outside. She is not a voluntary substitute and cannot be blamed for violence inflicted by the mob, host, Levite, city, or social order.
The verbs describe repeated sexual assault and abuse through the night. Translation should be direct enough to resist euphemism but must not sensationalize or turn her trauma into spectacle.

Her hands on the threshold preserve a final bodily image of seeking shelter from the house that failed her. Whether she died before or after the Levite's later action has been debated, but the received narrative gives no answering voice.

The command 'Arise, and let us go' displays profound callousness toward a catastrophically injured or dead woman. It must not be used to portray trauma survivors as unresponsive, disobedient, or spiritually deficient.

The Levite dismembers and distributes her body without recorded consent, transforming her again into an instrument for male political mobilization. National outrage does not restore her name, voice, or bodily integrity.

The summons frames the event as unprecedented outrage, yet the following chapters redirect grief into war and further violence against women. Moral shock without survivor-centered justice can become another engine of domination.
safety_note: Maximum anti-sexual-violence and anti-trauma safeguards: Do not blame the unnamed woman, eroticize the assault, excuse the host or Levite, or call her surrender a sacrifice. Her relationship status, possible estrangement, travel, sexuality, silence, injury, and death never authorize abuse. Do not preach that hospitality requires surrendering women, that male rape is uniquely shameful, or that collective revenge repairs sexual violence. Center her stolen safety, voice, body, and dignity, and refuse every use of this chapter against women, queer people, migrants, sex workers, trauma survivors, or any targeted group.
